name: Global Plastics Treaty (INC-5.4 Negotiations)
description: The UN Intergovernmental Negotiating Committee (INC) is working toward a legally binding global plastics treaty covering the full plastic lifecycle. After stalled negotiations at INC-5.1 (Busan, Nov 2024) and INC-5.2 (Geneva, Aug 2025) and the resignation of the chair, a new chair (Julio Cordano of Chile) was elected at the procedural INC-5.3 session on Feb 7, 2026. Substantive negotiations are expected to resume at INC-5.4 (date/location TBD, likely mid-2026).
whyNow: Negotiations are at a critical inflection point: a new chair has been elected after months of obstruction, and civil society is pushing hard for a reset with an ambitious, legally binding treaty that addresses plastic production, not just waste. The INC-5.4 session is the next major make-or-break moment before the process loses all momentum. Petrochemical industry and 'high-ambition' coalition states are in direct conflict.

ncrs.rationale: Broad public concern about plastic pollution translates well visually. High benefit visibility (ocean plastics, microplastics in food). Risk of conformity trap is moderate since negotiating positions are polarized. Broad angle potential is very high — touches ocean, health, climate, Global South justice, corporate accountability. Artist activation (e.g., Von Wong's work) fits naturally.

timeline.event: UNEA-5.2 adopts resolution to develop global plastics treaty; INC process launched
INC-4 in Ottawa; high-ambition coalition forms; petrochemical bloc obstructs
INC-5.1 in Busan collapses without agreement on production caps
INC-5.2 in Geneva also stalls; chair resigns
INC-5.3 (Feb 7, Geneva): procedural session elects new chair, Julio Cordano
INC-5.4: substantive negotiations resume (date/location TBD, mid-2026)

keyOrgs.name: High Ambition Coalition to End Plastic Pollution

regulatoryWindow: INC-5.4 (mid-2026, TBD) is the next formal negotiation session. Without progress, treaty momentum may collapse entirely. Window for civil society pressure is open NOW ahead of scheduling and text finalization.
slogans: No Plastic Produced = No Plastic Polluted

name: BBNJ High Seas Treaty — First COP
description: The UN High Seas Treaty (BBNJ Agreement — Biodiversity Beyond National Jurisdiction) entered into force on January 17, 2026, after 60 countries ratified. It creates the first legal framework to protect marine biodiversity in international waters covering ~50% of Earth's surface. The first Conference of Parties (COP1) must be held within one year of entry into force (i.e., by January 2027), with Preparatory Commission meetings in 2026 establishing institutional architecture.
whyNow: COP1 will set the rules for marine protected areas, environmental impact assessments, and benefit-sharing of marine genetic resources for decades. Civil society has a narrow window to shape the treaty's implementation mechanisms before they are locked in. The 30x30 ocean protection target (protecting 30% of the ocean by 2030) requires urgent designation of high seas MPAs starting now.

ncrs.rationale: Treaty is now law — 'we won, now what?' is a powerful mobilization frame. Benefit visibility is strong via ocean imagery. Conformity trap moderate (technocratic process can demobilize). Broad angle potential high — ocean covers climate, biodiversity, food security, Global South benefit-sharing, deep-sea mining opposition.

timeline.event: BBNJ Agreement text finalized in March after 20 years of negotiations
60th ratification reached September 19, 2025
Treaty enters into force January 17, 2026 — legally binding
PrepCom session 3: March 23–April 2, 2026 (Geneva) — institutional rules being set
BBNJ COP1: to be held by January 2027 — designates first high seas MPAs

regulatoryWindow: PrepCom 3 (March 23–April 2, 2026) and COP1 (by Jan 2027) are key institutional windows. MPA nominations and benefit-sharing rules being written NOW. Deep-sea mining moratorium advocacy is critical leverage point.

name: COP31 — Climate Justice & Fossil Fuel Phase-Out
description: The 31st UN Climate Conference (COP31) will be hosted in Antalya, Turkey, November 9–20, 2026, with Australia as President of Negotiations and a Pacific pre-COP co-hosted by Australia and Pacific island nations. This COP is the first major climate check-in after the 2025 Global Stocktake, and is expected to pressure countries to update their Nationally Determined Contributions (NDCs) for 2035 targets. The sequence of Global South/Pacific-facing COPs (Brazil 2025, Turkey/Pacific 2026, Africa 2027) creates momentum for climate justice framing.
whyNow: Australia's presidency creates tension: a coal-exporting nation leading climate negotiations. Civil society is mobilizing to demand Australia prove climate leadership through domestic fossil fuel phase-out, strong climate finance, and Pacific-centered ambition. The Loss and Damage Fund, NDC ambition, and fossil fuel non-proliferation treaty are key pressure points. Youth movements and Pacific island voices amplified.

ncrs.rationale: High tipping threshold — COP events reliably generate global media and street mobilization. Australia presidency contradiction is a potent narrative hook. Pacific islands provide high emotional stakes. Broad angle potential very high: fossil fuels, finance, justice, Indigenous rights. Fridays for Future, 350.org, AYCC, and Pacific Climate Warriors all active.

timeline.event: First Global Stocktake shows world off track on Paris Agreement targets
COP30 Belem, Brazil: updated NDCs due; Loss and Damage fund operationalized
Pre-COP in Pacific (date TBD): Pacific nations and Australia co-lead leaders' event
COP31 in Antalya, Turkey (Nov 9–20): NDC 2035 targets, climate finance, fossil fuel phase-out
COP32 in Africa: pressure on African climate finance and just transition

regulatoryWindow: NDC submission deadline (Feb 2025 passed; next major round 2035 targets due by COP31). Loss and Damage Fund capitalization, $1.3 trillion climate finance goal under NCQG are active negotiation items.

name: Loss and Damage Fund — Civil Society Mobilization
description: The Fund for Responding to Loss and Damage (FRLD), established at COP27 (2022) and operationalized at COP28, is the world's first dedicated mechanism to compensate developing nations for climate harms they did not cause. Civil society is demanding at least $400 billion/year in disbursements through the FRLD, and $724 billion in new grants under the New Collective Quantified Goal (NCQG) by 2030. The Baku to Belém Roadmap to $1.3 trillion in climate finance is the overarching goal.
whyNow: The FRLD Board is operationalizing direct access, governance, and eligibility rules right now in 2026. Early Board decisions will determine whether the fund reaches frontline communities or gets captured by multilateral bureaucracy. The Loss and Damage Youth Coalition (900+ members, 70+ countries) is a rising mobilization force. Climate litigation and human rights framing are converging with loss and damage advocacy.

ncrs.rationale: High penalty reduction — developing countries bearing existential climate costs are a potent injustice narrative. Benefit visibility strong (Pacific islands, Bangladesh, Small Island States). Youth coalition provides sustained energy. Broad angle: colonial justice, climate finance, accountability, human rights all converge.

timeline.event: COP27 establishes Loss and Damage Fund after decades of developing country advocacy
COP28 operationalizes fund; pledges total ~$800M (far below need)
FRLD Board begins work; civil society demands direct access and grant-based finance
COP30 Belem: Baku to Belém $1.3T roadmap agreed; NCQG includes L&D sub-goal
FRLD Board sessions: direct access rules, country eligibility, and first grants being decided
COP31 Antalya: pressure to scale capitalization to $400B/year target

keyOrgs.name: Fund for Responding to Loss and Damage (FRLD)

regulatoryWindow: FRLD Board sessions in 2026 are setting direct access rules and first disbursement mechanisms. COP31 (Nov 2026) is the next major capitalization pressure point. Window to embed civil society participation in Board governance is NOW.

name: WHO Pandemic Accord — Ratification & Implementation
description: The WHO Pandemic Agreement (Accord) was agreed in principle at the WHO Intergovernmental Negotiating Body (INB) in April 2025 and adopted at the World Health Assembly in May 2025. It is now in the ratification phase in 2026. Civil society celebrated the deal as a 'first step' but criticized diluted provisions on intellectual property, technology transfer, and access to medical countermeasures — key COVID-19 lessons. The accord must now be ratified by member states and implementation mechanisms developed.
whyNow: The accord's effectiveness depends entirely on how ambiguous provisions on IP waivers, pathogen sharing (PABS system), and equitable access are interpreted in implementing regulations. Big Pharma lobbied hard to weaken the text; now civil society must push governments to implement strongly. US withdrawal from negotiations (under Trump) has weakened the accord's enforcement architecture. Ratification race and implementation standard-setting are the active battlegrounds in 2026.

ncrs.rationale: COVID-19 remains in public memory; 'never again' framing resonates strongly. High penalty reduction — millions died due to vaccine inequity. Pharma vs. people framing is compelling. Conformity trap risk elevated in technocratic WHO process. Broad angle: health equity, pharmaceutical accountability, IP reform, Global South rights.

timeline.event: WHO member states launch negotiations for Pandemic Treaty post-COVID-19
US blocks progress; negotiations repeatedly stall on IP and access
April 2025: consensus text agreed at INB; US has withdrawn from process
May 2025: World Health Assembly adopts Pandemic Agreement
Ratification campaign by member states; PABS (pathogen sharing) implementing regulations
WHA79 (May 2026): review of implementation progress, pressure on ambiguous provisions

keyOrgs.name: People's Medicines Alliance (formerly People's Vaccine Alliance)

regulatoryWindow: WHA79 (May 2026) is the next major WHO governance moment. PABS implementing agreement negotiations are ongoing in 2026. Annex to the Accord on IP waiver mechanisms requires member state action — this is the active battleground.

name: Stop Killer Robots — Autonomous Weapons Treaty
description: The Stop Killer Robots coalition (274+ organizations) is campaigning for a binding international treaty to ban fully autonomous weapons systems ('killer robots') that can select and attack targets without human control. The UN General Assembly's First Committee adopted resolutions on autonomous weapons for three consecutive years (2023, 2024, 2025), with the November 2025 resolution expressing concern about 'consequences and impact of autonomous weapon systems.' A growing bloc of states has signaled readiness to start treaty negotiations, expected to accelerate in 2026.
whyNow: AI-driven autonomous targeting is being deployed in active conflicts (Gaza, Ukraine), making the issue viscerally urgent. A UNGA resolution in November 2025 showed growing state consensus. The UN Secretary-General has called these weapons 'morally repugnant.' The REAIM summit process and Conference on Disarmament (CD) are potential venues for negotiations in 2026. Civil society is pushing for formal mandate to negotiate a binding treaty — the window is 2026–2027.

ncrs.rationale: Extremely high penalty reduction — autonomous weapons represent existential threat to human dignity and civilian safety. Very high broad angle: AI ethics, military accountability, racial bias in targeting algorithms, international humanitarian law. Low conformity trap — movement has clear binary demand (ban/don't ban). High visual/emotional impact for activist art framing.

timeline.event: Stop Killer Robots coalition launched
UNGA First Committee adopts first-ever resolution on autonomous weapons systems
REAIM summit (Responsible AI in Military Domain); UNGA resolution adopted third year running (Nov 2025)
Conference on Disarmament sessions (Jan–March 2026 Geneva): states push for formal treaty mandate
Growing state bloc signals readiness for binding negotiations; UNGA vote expected again Nov 2026
Formal treaty negotiations potentially begin if state consensus reached

regulatoryWindow: Conference on Disarmament (Jan–Mar 2026, Geneva), First Committee UNGA (Oct–Nov 2026). 2026 is the critical year for escalating from resolutions to formal treaty negotiations mandate.
slogans: Keep a Human In The Loop

name: CSW70 — Women's Access to Justice
description: The 70th Session of the Commission on the Status of Women (CSW70), held March 9–19, 2026 at UN Headquarters, is the UN's largest annual gathering on gender equality. The priority theme is 'Ensuring and strengthening access to justice for all women and girls, including by promoting inclusive and equitable legal systems, eliminating discriminatory laws, policies, and practices, and addressing structural barriers.' Over 10,000 delegates from government, civil society, and NGOs attend. Agreed Conclusions from CSW70 will guide global gender policy.
whyNow: The session happens at a moment of sharp global backlash against gender rights — rollback of abortion rights, femicide crises in Latin America, rollback of LGBTQ+ protections. The 'access to justice' theme directly engages legal system reform, discriminatory laws, and structural barriers. The review theme (women's participation in public life from CSW65) creates accountability for 5-year commitments. This is a prime moment for feminist activists to set the global agenda.

ncrs.rationale: High penalty reduction — impunity for gender-based violence and discriminatory laws affect billions. Strong cross-regional solidarity (Latin America femicide, South Asian child marriage, African FGM, Western abortion rights rollback). Broad angle bridges legal reform, economic justice, healthcare access, climate vulnerability. Conformity trap risk via over-formalization.

timeline.event: CSW65: Agreed Conclusions on women's participation in public life — 5-year review at CSW70
Femicide crisis in Latin America; Dobbs decision aftermath in US; global feminist organizing
CSW70 (March 9–19, 2026, New York): largest annual global gathering on gender equality
Agreed Conclusions adopted — global policy commitments on justice systems

keyOrgs.name: DAWN (Development Alternatives with Women for a New Era)
AWID (Association for Women's Rights in Development)

regulatoryWindow: CSW70 Agreed Conclusions (adopted March 19, 2026) establish 5-year global policy benchmarks on women's access to justice. Implementation reviews begin immediately. Key window to embed strong language on reproductive rights, GBV legal accountability, and Indigenous women's rights.

name: UN Permanent Forum on Indigenous Issues (UNPFII 25) — Health in Conflict
description: The 25th Session of the UN Permanent Forum on Indigenous Issues (UNPFII 25) meets April 20–May 1, 2026 at UN Headquarters with the theme 'Ensuring Indigenous Peoples' health, including in the context of conflict.' This brings together Indigenous delegates, member states, and UN agencies to address Indigenous health rights intersecting with armed conflict, climate displacement, and territorial dispossession. Pre-sessional expert group meeting (Dec 2025) focused on nomadic and semi-nomadic peoples, climate crisis, and biodiversity governance.
whyNow: Multiple ongoing crises intersect: Gaza conflict, Sudan, Myanmar, and Canada residential school health legacy. The theme explicitly connects health, conflict, and territorial rights — a powerful convergence for Indigenous peoples facing extractive industries and climate displacement simultaneously. UNPFII recommendations carry formal status in UN system and can influence BBNJ, CBD, UNFCCC, and INC plastics processes.

ncrs.rationale: Very high penalty reduction — Indigenous communities face disproportionate colonial health harms, conflict violence, and climate impacts. Broad angle: health equity, territorial rights, Free Prior Informed Consent (FPIC), biodiversity, mining rights. Tipping threshold moderate — UNPFII less public-facing than COPs but high advocacy leverage.

timeline.event: UN Declaration on the Rights of Indigenous Peoples (UNDRIP) adopted
Kunming-Montreal GBF includes Indigenous Peoples in biodiversity governance
Pre-sessional expert group meeting on climate/biodiversity/territorial rights (Dec 2025, virtual)
UNPFII 25 (April 20–May 1, 2026): Health + conflict theme with formal UN recommendations
Recommendations feed into CBD COP, BBNJ COP1, and HLPF SDG reviews

keyOrgs.name: UN Permanent Forum on Indigenous Issues (UNPFII)
International Indigenous Peoples Forum on Climate Change (IIPFCC)

regulatoryWindow: UNPFII 25 recommendations (May 2026) formally submitted to ECOSOC and UN agencies. These influence the BBNJ PrepCom, CBD COP, and HLPF SDG reviews in 2026. Free Prior Informed Consent (FPIC) operationalization in extractive and climate projects is the key battleground.

name: Global Digital Compact — AI Governance Implementation
description: The Global Digital Compact (GDC), adopted at the UN Summit of the Future (September 2024), is a comprehensive framework for digital cooperation and AI governance. The UN Office for Digital and Emerging Technologies (ODET) is now implementing it, including the AI Governance framework. 2026 is the first full year of GDC implementation, with civil society pushing for meaningful inclusion in AI governance forums, regulation of surveillance technologies, and bridging the digital divide.
whyNow: The GDC's AI provisions are being operationalized exactly as generative AI's social impacts are peaking. The ITU's AI for Good Global Summit (July 7–10, 2026, Geneva) is a major convergence point. The gap between GDC commitments on digital inclusion and surveillance state realities in the Global South is a growing pressure point. Civil society is fighting to prevent GDC from becoming a pro-industry document.

ncrs.rationale: High conformity trap risk — AI governance processes tend toward technocratic capture. Broad angle potential very high: affects labor, surveillance, democracy, education, healthcare, military. Benefit visibility moderate but improving as AI impacts become tangible. Penalty reduction strong re: surveillance, algorithmic bias, digital colonialism.

timeline.event: World Summit on Information Society (WSIS) — 20-year review due in 2025
Global Digital Compact adopted at Summit of the Future (September 22, 2024)
WSIS+20 review process; ODET established as UN AI governance body
AI for Good Global Summit (July 7–10, 2026, Geneva): first major GDC implementation review
Internet Governance Forum 2026: civil society input on GDC implementation
GDC first progress review at UN General Assembly

keyOrgs.name: UN Office for Digital and Emerging Technologies (ODET)

regulatoryWindow: GDC implementation began January 2026. AI for Good Summit (July 2026) is first major accountability checkpoint. Internet Governance Forum 2026 will feature civil society GDC monitoring. Key window to shape AI governance norms before they calcify.

name: UNCCD COP17 — Land Degradation and Desertification
description: The 17th Conference of the Parties to the UN Convention to Combat Desertification (UNCCD COP17) will be held in Ulaanbaatar, Mongolia, August 17–28, 2026. It focuses on land degradation, drought resilience, and desertification affecting over 3.2 billion people globally. This COP arrives at a critical moment as the UNCCD's Land Degradation Neutrality (LDN) targets approach the 2030 deadline and droughts intensify from climate change.
whyNow: The global land degradation crisis affects more people than any other environmental crisis yet receives far less funding and attention than climate or biodiversity. Mongolia hosting shifts the spotlight to Central Asian nomadic communities and the devastating impact of dzud (extreme cold and snow events). Civil society is pushing for recognition of land rights, pastoralist communities, and the connection between land degradation and forced migration.

ncrs.rationale: Lower profile than climate/biodiversity COPs but strong regional significance. Mongolia hosting creates unique opportunity for Central Asian/nomadic peoples' rights framing. Broad angle: food security, migration, Indigenous land rights, climate adaptation. Penalty reduction strong for pastoral communities facing existential threat.

timeline.event: Land Degradation Neutrality (LDN) targets set as part of SDG 15
UNCCD COP15 in Abidjan: 'Land is Life' declaration, drought protocol negotiations begin
UNCCD COP16 in Riyadh: drought resilience framework and land rights advocacy
UNCCD COP17 in Ulaanbaatar, Mongolia (Aug 17–28): LDN progress review, drought treaty, pastoralist rights
LDN 2030 targets deadline — Land Degradation Neutrality must be achieved

regulatoryWindow: COP17 (Aug 2026) is the primary decision point for UNCCD drought protocol finalization, LDN progress review, and land rights commitments. Mongolia hosting creates unique opportunity for Central Asian nomadic communities' rights advocacy.

name: HLPF 2026 — SDG Accountability (Water, Energy, Cities)
description: The High-level Political Forum on Sustainable Development (HLPF 2026) meets July 6–15, 2026 at UN Headquarters under the theme 'Transformative, equitable, innovative and coordinated actions for the 2030 Agenda.' It will conduct in-depth reviews of SDGs 6 (water), 7 (energy), 9 (infrastructure/innovation), 11 (cities), and 17 (partnerships). 36 countries will present Voluntary National Reviews (VNRs). With 4 years to the 2030 SDG deadline, this is a critical accountability moment.
whyNow: The SDG midpoint (2023 SDG Summit) showed the world drastically off track — only 17% of SDG targets are on course. HLPF 2026 is the major follow-up accountability moment specifically for water, energy, and cities — three SDGs critical to climate adaptation and urban equity. Civil society is using this to push accountability for governments' broken promises and advocate for SDG-aligned financing.

ncrs.rationale: HLPF tends toward diplomatic process capture, hence high conformity trap risk. However, SDG accountability framing has strong resonance with development NGOs, youth movements, and Global South advocates. Water and cities themes have clear benefit visibility. Four years to 2030 creates urgency framing.

timeline.event: SDGs adopted as part of 2030 Agenda
SDG Summit at UNGA: midpoint review shows 17% of targets on track
HLPF 2026 (July 6–15, NY): in-depth reviews of SDG 6,7,9,11,17; 36 country VNRs
SDG Summit 2027 at UNGA: major accountability moment
SDG deadline — global assessment of progress

regulatoryWindow: HLPF 2026 Ministerial Declaration (July 15, 2026) and country VNRs create accountability benchmarks. SDG Investment Fair (April 20–24, 2026) and ECOSOC Youth Forum (April 14–16, 2026) are pre-HLPF mobilization moments.

name: Universal Periodic Review (UPR) — Human Rights Accountability Cycles
description: The Universal Periodic Review (UPR) is the UN mechanism reviewing every country's human rights record in rotating cycles. In 2026, three sessions (January, May, November) will review 42 countries including Australia, France, Germany, Saudi Arabia, and many Global South states. Civil society organizations submit 'stakeholder reports' that directly influence state recommendations. UPR findings are used by advocates to hold governments accountable domestically.
whyNow: With global human rights backsliding — from LGBTQ+ rights rollbacks to crackdowns on environmental defenders — UPR 2026 provides concrete leverage mechanisms. Australia under review in January 2026 (as it holds COP31 presidency) creates a unique accountability moment. CSOs are coordinating to use UPR recommendations on climate, Indigenous rights, and gender to pressure governments with formal UN findings.

ncrs.rationale: Strong penalty reduction mechanism — UPR creates formal UN accountability for human rights violations. Lower tipping threshold as UPR is less visible to public. High broad angle — covers climate rights, Indigenous rights, gender, civil liberties, and economic rights under one mechanism. Conformity trap risk of over-institutionalization.

timeline.event: UPR mechanism established by UN Human Rights Council
UPR Session 51 (January 2026): Australia, Austria, Georgia, Lebanon, Nepal, Rwanda reviewed
UPR Session 52 (May 2026): Saudi Arabia, France, Germany and others under review
UPR Session 53 (November 2026): Another 14 states reviewed

keyOrgs.name: Global Coalition to Protect Education from Attack (GCPEA)

regulatoryWindow: UPR stakeholder submissions due 6-8 months before each session. January 2026 session (Australia under review): leverage moment given COP31 presidency. May 2026 session: key G7 countries. All three 2026 sessions are active civil society engagement windows.
